title: Isaiah 30
subtitle: Woe to the Obstinate Children

sections.header: Verses 1–17 — “In Repentance and Rest Is Your Salvation”

heb.paragraph: "In repentance (shūbâ) and rest is your salvation, in quietness and trust is your strength, but you would have none of it" (v.15). Woe to the obstinate children who carry out plans not from God, who go down to Egypt for help without consulting the LORD (vv.1–2). Egypt’s help is utterly useless (v.7). They tell the prophets: "Stop confronting us with the Holy One of Israel!" (v.11). They reject the word and trust in oppression and deceit (v.12). Their wall will collapse suddenly, in an instant — shattered like pottery (vv.13–14). Then v.15 — one of the most important verses in Isaiah: salvation is in returning and resting, strength is in quietness and trusting. But they refused.

notes.note: "Woe to the obstinate children who carry out plans that are not mine. They go down to Egypt for help." The political sin: trusting Egypt’s military instead of God’s promise.
"Tell us pleasant things, prophesy illusions. Stop confronting us with the Holy One of Israel!" The demand for comfortable preaching. The refusal to hear truth.

"In repentance and rest is your salvation, in quietness and trust is your strength, but you would have none of it." The prescription: return, rest, be quiet, trust. The diagnosis: you refused. The four verbs are passive — they require stopping, not striving.

refs.note: The parallel woe against relying on Egypt — horses and chariots versus the Holy One.
'Some trust in chariots, some in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD' — the same polemic.
The king must not acquire many horses or return to Egypt — Isaiah's critique has Torah roots.
Ephraim 'like a dove, easily deceived' — calling to Egypt, going to Assyria.

hist.context: The embassy to Egypt (vv.1-7) sought military alliance against Assyria — the sensible political option. Isaiah condemns it as trusting 'Rahab' (Egypt, v.7) who sits still, powerless. The command to 'write it on a tablet' (v.8) preserves the prophecy for future vindication. The people want prophets who speak 'smooth things' (v.10), not uncomfortable truths about trust in Yahweh alone. The destruction described (vv.12-14) matches Sennacherib's campaign.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the irony: the plan is 'not mine' (not Yahweh's), adding 'sin to sin.' Each diplomatic move away from trust in God compounds guilt. Egypt's help is worthless — a 'Rahab who sits still.'
The tablet command (v.8) creates a witness for future generations. When the disaster comes, they'll know it was predicted. Oswalt sees this as Isaiah creating his own archive of vindication.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the Egypt-alliance critique as programmatic for Isaiah's political theology: security comes from trust in Yahweh, not international alliances. This challenges every generation's temptation to trust in human power.
The demand for 'smooth things' and 'illusions' (v.10) names a permanent temptation. Childs notes that every generation has false prophets who tell people what they want to hear.

sections.header: Verses 18–33 — “The LORD Longs to Be Gracious to You”

heb.gloss: to be gracious / to show favour
heb.paragraph: "Yet the LORD longs to be gracious (ḥānan) to you; therefore he will rise up to show you compassion" (v.18). After the refusal of v.15, God STILL longs to be gracious. The bread of adversity and the water of affliction will come, but "your Teacher will be hidden no more; your eyes will see your Teacher" (v.20). "Whether you turn to the right or to the left, your ears will hear a voice behind you, saying, ‘This is the way; walk in it’" (v.21). The guidance promise: a voice BEHIND you — following, not leading from a distance. Idols will be discarded (v.22). Rain and abundant harvest (vv.23–25). The moon will shine like the sun, and the sun seven times brighter — "when the LORD binds up the bruises of his people" (v.26).

notes.note: "The LORD longs to be gracious to you; he rises to show you compassion." God LONGS. The verb is ḥākah — waiting eagerly, not passively. God is impatient to show mercy.

"Your Teacher will be hidden no more. Your ears will hear a voice behind you: This is the way; walk in it." The guidance verse. A voice BEHIND — like a shepherd following the flock, not a general commanding from the front.

"The moon will shine like the sun, and the sunlight will be seven times brighter, when the LORD binds up the bruises of his people." Cosmic healing: light multiplied. The binding of bruises produces a seven-fold sun.

refs.note: 'Be still and know that I am God' — the same quietness and trust Isaiah commends.
'If you do not stand firm in faith, you will not stand at all' — the earlier trust oracle.
'It is good to wait quietly for the salvation of the LORD' — Lamentations echoes Isaiah's counsel.
The rider on the white horse — the divine warrior Isaiah 30:27-33 anticipates.

hist.context: The famous verse 'in quietness and trust is your strength' (v.15) summarizes Isaiah's political theology. The 'theophany' section (vv.27-33) depicts Yahweh coming in storm and fire to defeat Assyria — the 'rod' that struck Israel now broken by a greater rod. 'Topheth' (v.33) is the valley where child sacrifice occurred; now it becomes the pyre for Assyria's king. The reversal is complete: the oppressor meets the fate he imposed on others.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt sees v.15 as the theological climax: 'in returning and rest you shall be saved; in quietness and trust is your strength.' This isn't passivity but active trust — refusing human solutions to wait for divine action.

The theophany imagery is overwhelming: breath like a torrent, sieve of destruction, every stroke of the rod accompanied by music. Oswalt notes the ironic joy: Assyria's fall becomes Israel's festival.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs considers this verse the hermeneutical key to Isaiah's political theology. 'Quietness and trust' is not apathy but the active refusal to secure oneself by human means. It requires more faith than fighting.

The 'waiting' theme connects to chapter 8: those who wait for Yahweh are the true disciples. Childs notes the eschatological expansion: waiting leads to healing, abundant provision, and cosmic transformation.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: "The LORD longs to be gracious to you…

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: Stop confronting us with the Holy One of Israel!" The demand for comfortable preaching…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVwho go down to Egypt without consulting me; who look for help to Pharaoh’s protection, to Egypt’s shade for refuge.ESVwho set out to go down to Egypt, without asking for my direction, to take refuge in the protection of Pharaoh and to seek shelter in the shadow of Egypt!NIVBut Pharaoh’s protection will be to your shame, Egypt’s shade will bring you disgrace.ESVTherefore shall the protection of Pharaoh turn to your shame, and the shelter in the shadow of Egypt to your humiliation.

rec.text: ""Woe to the obstinate children who carry out plans that are not mine. They go down to Egypt for help." The political sin: trusting Egypt’s military instead of God’s promise."

rows.label: God Longs — 30:18–33
rows.text: "The LORD LONGS to be gracious." "This is the WAY; WALK in it." Sun seven times brighter when he binds the bruises.

hebtext.note: "In repentance (shūbâ) and rest is your salvation, in quietness and trust is your strength, but you would have none of it" (v.15). Woe to the obstinate children who carry out plans not from God, who go down to Egypt for help without consulting the LORD (vv.1–2). Egypt’s help is utterly useless (v.7). They tell the prophets: "Stop confronting us with the Holy One of Israel!" (v.11). They reject the word and trust in oppression and deceit (v.12). Their wall will collapse suddenly, in an instant — shattered like pottery (vv.13–14). Then v.15 — one of the most important verses in Isaiah: salvation is in returning and resting, strength is in quietness and trusting. But they refused.

hebtext.gloss: to be gracious / to show favour
hebtext.note: "Yet the LORD longs to be gracious (ḥānan) to you; therefore he will rise up to show you compassion" (v.18). After the refusal of v.15, God STILL longs to be gracious. The bread of adversity and the water of affliction will come, but "your Teacher will be hidden no more; your eyes will see your Teacher" (v.20). "Whether you turn to the right or to the left, your ears will hear a voice behind you, saying, ‘This is the way; walk in it’" (v.21). The guidance promise: a voice BEHIND you — following, not leading from a distance. Idols will be discarded (v.22). Rain and abundant harvest (vv.23–25). The moon will shine like the sun, and the sun seven times brighter — "when the LORD binds up the bruises of his people" (v.26).

themes.note: Mercy AND Faith both peak at 9: God LONGS to be gracious; salvation is in repentance and rest. The guidance voice: "this is the way." Sun seven times brighter.

coaching.tip: 'In repentance and rest is your salvation, in quietness and trust is your strength, but you would have none of it' (v. 15). This may be Isaiah's most devastating verse — not because the demand is harsh but because it's gentle. God offers rest; they choose horses. The tragedy of Judah's alliance with Egypt isn't that it's sinful in some abstract sense but that it substitutes frantic activity for the offered peace.

'The LORD longs to be gracious to you' (v. 18). The Hebrew verb for 'longs' (chakah) suggests patient waiting. God doesn't just tolerate repentance — he actively desires it, waits for it, delays judgment to create space for it. This verse dismantles the caricature of the wrathful Old Testament God. Isaiah's God is eager to forgive, held back only by the people's refusal to return.

prayer_prompt: As the LORD tells Judah 'in repentance and rest is your salvation, in quietness and trust is your strength,' consider asking God what restless striving He is inviting you to lay down today.
